chunks.text: And Andrew for being here. Uh, with that, I'm happy to pass off to you to make some general introductory remarks. Thank you. Time minister. Um, just to introduce myself to, uh, to everybody, I'm Andrew Clark, director General of the GCSB. Uh, with me today is Andrew Hampton, director General of the SIS. Uh, we'll both make some opening, uh, statements, and then we're happy to take questions.

Excellent. Um, this is my first time appearing before the, uh, committee having taken up the position, uh, just in October. Um, thanks for the opportunity to update you today on the work of the GCSB. We're New Zealand's lead organization for signals intelligence and Cybersecurity. Cybersecurity. We support our customers resilience and decision making to successfully navigate a disrupted and contested international environment.

Uh, it is the most challenging global security environment in a generation. In recent years, we have observed a sharp rise in geostrategic competition and global tensions, including armed conflict, which has driven supply chain disruption, global economic uncertainty, and the erosion of the rules based order that is fundamental to a small trading nation such as New Zealand.

In this environment, security threats evolve quickly and cross borders flexibly presenting challenges for both the bureau and the service. In the past year, we provided signals intelligence to customers and 18 government agencies and their ministers to equip decision makers with greater insight.

Our intelligence insights cover the entire range of national security intelligence priorities set by the government. Our second collection is primarily focused offshore, including our own region, which has been an enduring focus. The geostrategic competition we see playing out globally.

Uh, we're also witnessing in our neighborhood and it's our mission to understand what's happening. At the same time, we are working more closely with our domestic partners at home, particularly police and the SIS around counter-terrorism and violent extremism. In response to the Royal Commission of Inquiry into the Christchurch terror attacks, over the past year, our intelligence has helped to disrupt foreign interference and espionage operations, transnational criminal endeavors, including drugs, drug smuggling, plus terrorist,

and violent extremist threats. We also make a significant contribution to global counter-terrorism efforts, including disruption of attack planning. GCSB intelligence plays an important role in ensuring the safety of New Zealanders offshore, including deployed members of the New Zealand Defense Force, some of whom are in conflict zones.

And while I've spoken mainly about our own intelligence insights, we're also the conduit for partner intelligence to our decision makers, and particularly from five i's partners. I'll now move on to our cybersecurity mission, which is delivered through the National Cybersecurity Center Cybersecurity.

Under this function, the GCSB works to protect information systems critical to New Zealand's national interests from sophisticated security threats. In the last fiscal year, the NCSC recorded 316 cybersecurity incidents involving New Zealand's most nationally significant organizations.

These incidents represent the most serious malicious cyber activity targeting New Zealand. This figure is made up of a slightly higher proportion at 28% of financially motivated activity. Then, uh, that with links to state sponsored cyber actors at 23%, but broadly in line with previous years, while criminal groups predominantly target victims for financial gain through ransomware attacks, state sponsored cyber actors typically aim to maintain a covert presence on computer networks of high intelligence value.

Today, we publicly released information regarding intrusion into New Zealand's parliamentary networks by cyber actors sponsored by the People's Republic of China. This is the third time in the past year that we have publicly linked malicious state backed cyber activity to the PRC, uh, in that same timeframe.

We have also called out the Russian Federation on three occasions, the type of state backed techniques we're observing continue to evolve and test cyber security agencies the world over, including methods such as, uh, living off the land, which is the stealthy compromise of critical infrastructure, using legitimate tools on victim devices and networks to maintain access to significant targets without detection.

Our capability to defend New Zealand against these threats is expanding through the use of new capabilities and partnerships on top of our Cortex suite of services. The ncsc Malware Free Networks service provides realtime detection and disruption delivered through third party cybersecurity providers, and it is significantly increasing New Zealand's capability to disrupt malicious cyber activity.

The number of Kiwis protected by malware free networks, or MFN as it's now known, continues to grow. And today, our partnerships with third party providers in New Zealand mean that the vast majority of New Zealanders and New Zealand organizations now have MFN cover. That's a significant achievement in snowballing in success in a short time since its formal launch.

In November, 2021, MFN has disrupted more than 6.2 million malicious cyber events, and its positive impact was recognized with the Public Service Commission's Spirit of service award and the overall Prime Minister's award last year, uh, as well as industry recognition as the best security product award at the annual Information Security Awards, uh, ICE A NS in 2022.

Overall, our combined cyber defenses have helped mitigate an estimated $382 million worth of harm to New Zealand systems since 2016. Of course, the true value of our national cyber defense, uh, goes far beyond the dollar figure, uh, with business continuity, investment confidence and protection of Kiwi's data at the heart of our service.

In July last year, the government announced the integration of New Zealand's computer emergency response team or CERT NZ with the NCSE to create a single lead operational cybersecurity agency for New Zealand. Following the initial transfer of cert functions to the GCSB, we're now working to provide a stronger cybersecurity system and improved customer, uh, service for all New Zealanders.

While the NCSC customers include government departments, key economic generators, research institutions and operators of critical national infrastructure cert supports a broader range of businesses, uh, organizations and individuals who are affected by cybersecurity incidents.

cert cybersecurity outreach also goes beyond our borders Through its Pacific Partnership Program, CERT works with our close specific neighbors to build local cybersecurity resilience by providing advice and support. As Director General, I'm also the government chief Information Security Officer, or GCI o.

Last year cabinet confirmed a refreshed GCI o uh, mandate to strengthen public sector cyber standards and expand the GCI O mandate beyond the core public service to include crown agents. As the cyber threats we face evolve, so too must the public sector's approach to addressing those risks as our critical infrastructure is increasingly digitized.

I would also like to provide an update on the construction of the $300 million, all of government data center being built at RNZA base Auckland for Novi. The GCSB is the government lead for information security will operate this facility on behalf of the broad range of government agencies that will use it.

The facility will shore up the resilience of our secure data storage for the next quarter century workers on track with the vertical infrastructure taking shape, and the facility is due to be switched on early next year. Finally, uh, we are an agency that must always stay one technological step ahead of those who would do us harm, and we need to be alert to what lies around the corner, which includes risks associated with emerging technologies such as artificial intelligence and quantum computing.

That's not something that we can do alone. Uh, our international partnerships, uh, and particularly the five eyes are critical to our ability to adapt quickly. But our greatest foundation for success lies with the talented people who make up the GCSB, their specialist skills and the diversity of thought that they bring.

They're a slice of New Zealand that enables us to make sense of the security environment around us. They, along with many others, have also helped to build the more robust and trusted authorizing environment that we now work within. We are acutely aware that we can only be as effective as the trust that New Zealanders place in this, uh, thank you.

I'll now hand over to Andrew. Thank you, Andrew Koto. Uh, thank you for the opportunity to address the committee today. Next month will mark a year since I became director General Nzs. IS It has been a real privilege to lead this organization, particularly to witness firsthand the kind of work our people do each day to keep New Zealand and New Zealanders safe and secure.

Our team is driven by the ethos that we partner with others for impact. This will be a key theme of my comments today, but first, I'll brief you on my agency's insights on the current threats facing New Zealand and how we deliver impact through threat detection, deterrence and disruption.

Countering violent extremism and terrorism is an enduring priority for us and continues to occupy around half of our investigative time with the remainder dominated by foreign interference in espionage. New Zealand's national terrorism threat level currently stands at low.

That means we consider a, an attack to be a realistic possibility, but we are not seeing specific attack planning at this time. To be clear, it does not mean there is no threat. There is enough bio rhetoric online to suggest there are people who may have the motivation to launch an attack, and there are a small number of nonviolent extremists who continue to draw a significant investigative resource.

Five years on the Christchurch attacks still cast a long shadow and continue to inspire identity motivated violent extremists here and overseas. But there has also been a range of positive improvements in the past five years. In terms of the way Nzsis operates, there is more emphasis on threat discovery, including through increased online capability, much improved interagency collaboration, and very genuine community engagement.

The memories of the 51 shoe ha are never far from our thoughts in carrying out this important work. Across our counter-terrorism function investigative effort is fairly evenly split between faith identity and politically motivated groups and individuals.

However, we are seen in emerging trend where violent extremists are motivated by highly personalized ideologies with no strong allegiance to a specific violent extremist cause or group. Indeed, it is looking like the those with fluid I ideological convictions could become our most prominent subjects of investigation.

We're very live to the potential impact of the conflicts in Gaza and the Red Sea. Both internationally and domestically, communities with connections to the region are feeling both vulnerable and threatened. And this is compounded by the volume of hate spread online.

We also know that internationally the conflicts are being used by terrorist organizations to drive radicalization and recruitment. There is the possibility that these conflicts could provide a catalyst for motivating someone in New Zealand to undertake an identity politically or faith motivated attack.

However, we assess the greatest risk domestically, the spontaneous violence driven by emotion rather than a terrorist act. Countering violent extremist threats is a complex challenge, but it's important that I inform the committee that there has been successes.

I can't provide specific details in this forum, but there have been a number of instances over the past five years where our team working with others have detected and disrupted terrorist threats. Threats. The foreign interference in an espionage environment is equally complex and fast evolving, driven by geostrategic competition.

Last August, N-Z-S-I-S published an unclassified threat assessment, which included more detail than we have released before about the nature of foreign interference and espionage in New Zealand. It's been a game changer in terms of helping make the threat more real for those potentially affected by this kind of activity.

But we can't just identify the threats. We are equally focused on helping organize man organizations manage the risk, including through a range of publicly available advice. The annual assessment will be published in August of this year. The next one, foreign interference has the potential to corrode our democratic institutions, damage our economic prosperity and compromise New Zealand's safety, security, and human rights.

In New Zealand, we see state actors or their proxies seeking to covertly build long-term influence with individuals. In our national political environment, local government is becoming a more common target. Other business, our business community is increasingly susceptible to including state sponsored attempts to steal hard earned intellectual property or sensitive information.

The monitoring and harassment of ethnic communities is becoming more sophisticated and even more insidious. Foreign states like the People's Republic of China or the Republic of Iran, have always have ways to make life difficult for those who dare speak out against those governments.

That's simply unacceptable in a country like New Zealand. When I reference those countries, I'm talking specifically about the state and those who act on its behalf, not the Chinese or the Iranian people as a whole, and certainly not community members in New Zealand. In most cases, there's those very communities we need to protect at a global level.

New Zealanders have been drawn into a wider PRC strategy to strengthen the capability and lethality of its people's republic, its people's liberation army. Over the past 18 months, N-Z-S-I-S has identified seven New Zealand citizens who are contracted by a third party party to support military aviation training to the People's Liberation Army.

The training and expertise they were passing on was gained through previous employment with partner militaries and with the New Zealand Defense Force. Such activity clearly poses a major national security risk, and it is not in New Zealand's interest to have former military personnel training in other military who does not share the same values as our own.

I consider it as important that this kind of activity is called out after threat mitigation activities by N-Z-S-I-S and the introduction of legislation in partner countries. All seven individuals have recently left their training roles, but the risk remains that other New Zealanders may be lured to high salaries to replace them.

Prior to the general election, our agencies worked with the electoral commission and other election participants, including political parties and candidates to increase their awareness of threats and how to mitigate them. As New Zealanders would expect that N-Z-S-I-S conducted proactive threat discovery work during the period around the general election to detect signs of foreign interference.

Our assessment is that it's highly unlikely foreign state activity affected the outcome of the vote. However, we assessed, there were almost certainly several foreign states using the occasion of the election as an opportunity to conduct long-term influence.

Building the activity we detected was highly likely aimed at building influence with political candidates and parties, which is easier to do during the election period when they are more accessible. It is also unlikely the election was the target of any large state backed disinformation campaign, which is consistent with the nature of the disinformation environment discussed in our unclassified assessment.

I know that when we paint a picture of the threat environment in isolation, it can look rather bleak. What is more positive is there is great work, there is a great deal of work happening, most of it in concert with others that is placing us ahead of the thread. Last October, I stood on a stage in California with New Zealand's five eyes partners to warn against the threat of economic espionage.

At the same time, we jointly promoted a set of secure innovation principles that any organization can use to protect their precious intellectual property from those who wish to steal it. Those principles can apply equally to any New Zealand innovator as much as they do to Silicon Valley.

Our work also frequently involves supporting Pacific partners to build their own national security resilience. Just this month, our Cook Islands partners put in place their own protective security policy framework for government modeled closely on NZ SI's protective security requirements. There is a lot we do with the New Zealand public too.

Our guide for identifying signs of violent extremism reminds is relevant now as when it was published 16 months ago. If the public can tell us when they see some of the behaviors and activities outlined in the guide, then I have no doubt that more attack plans will be disruptive alongside the GCSB.

We have recently published our organizational strategies for the coming year on our websites. If we are to continue building deeper partnerships with a wide range of New Zealanders, it is important that we are clear about who we are as organizations and what we seek to achieve for New Zealand.

Uh, nor of course, happy to take questions. What Can I say to Andrew And Andrew? Thank you so much for those introductory remarks. Um, we'll open it up to the committee in questions. Now we need to hard stop at, um, 5 55 and I guess the first question, if you don't mind, I'll just ask around, you know, obviously acknowledging the public attribution of malicious cyber activity, uh, Andrew Clark, uh, today would've become as a bit of a surprise to New Zealanders.

Um, listening to that, uh, why was it so important, uh, for us to go public, uh, with that information and know we've been increasingly doing so, I guess, uh, uh, in the previous administration on this administration as well. Yeah, thanks, prime Minister. Um, of course, the decision to, uh, to go public is always, um, one, um, that it's made by government in the national, uh, interest.

But, uh, increasingly we find ourselves, uh, having to, uh, speak out, um, in support of rules-based international, uh, sorry, international, uh, norms of responsible behavior and international, uh, rules, uh, in this case, um, uh, there are a large number of countries, uh, that understand what those international norms of responsible behavior are in cyberspace.

Uh, and so, uh, what we wanted to do was to link up with like-minded, uh, partners who are, uh, willing to speak out in the same time and space about the same issue of what they're experiencing. Great. Chris? Yeah, I was interested in, um, your conversation there around people with fluid I ideological convictions and the fact that these individuals could pose greater risk in the future, particularly around spontaneous violence driven by emotion.

Does, doesn't that make it harder to identify who those people are? Because if there, if, if there's no particular ideology driving it, how would we possibly identify them? Um, um, that's exactly right, Mr. You know, when you're talking about what we sometimes refer to as networked terrorist organizations, you know, someone who may be affiliated with an organization offshore or even inspired by them, it's sometimes possible notwithstanding encrypted communication apps and the like to, to essentially, um, follow them by following the, the organization.

When you have people shopping around for different ideologies, that's much harder. And it's, um, a key key challenge for agencies like ours because we don't undertake mass surveillance. So just to let you know, what we do do, um, yes, we do online operations, we do horizons scanning, but we also rely really, um, heavily on the public because often it's the public who's going to see someone exhibiting those concerning behaviors and they'll, they'll be able to alert us.

So that's why we put those indicators out, um, about a year and a half ago. So, so I guess, what are the sort of trigger points that you might see? So if it is as genuinely spontaneous violence driven by emotion, I wrote that down because that kind of alarmed me a little bit. What would be the things that might tip someone into that situation?

I think if it's not an ideology, I, I, I think we've, we've run two things together. So the, um, first point was around ideological motivation where yes, we are definitely seeing people shopping around between different ideologies. Some common trends are they're quite young, sometimes they've had quite disrupted upbringings and are marginalized, and unfortunately they're obsessed with, with violence.

The point around spontaneous violence, what I was, um, attempting to convey there is not withstanding, um, that impact of the war in, in Gaza and the way in which that has led to heightened emotions domestically and led to those communities feeling vulnerable.

We don't get a sense that there's anyone mobilizing to ideological violence or faith-based violence. But I guess what we're concerned about, and certainly our police colleagues are, is with increased protest, with increased, um, coming together of, of different groups with opposed views.

There may be spontaneous violence there, but it wouldn't, that wouldn't meet the threshold of, of terrorism for us. So, I'm sorry I was, they were separate points that I would have run Together. Just one more for me, 'cause I'm aware that there'll be others Wanna ask questions as well. You, in your conversation of the election, you mentioned sort of evidence that there were people trying to build relationships with candidates and parties.

To what extent did you alert the candidates and parties that that was happening? Bearing them mind? That's probably the greatest safeguard against that kind of behavior. Um, A a again, um, Mr. Kin, I, I agree completely with what you have said. You know, when we talk about foreign affairs, we're not talking about people trying to build relationships with the mps.

We are not even talking about governments and open ways trying to influence New Zealand policy. What we are talking about is behavior that's undertaken in, in deceptive covert corruptive ways. So often the person who is the target, indeed, in most cases, they are unwitting, they may not know that the person making the donation to them or the person seeking to meet with them has an ulterior motive.

So what we do is generally provide briefings to political parties, to candidates on what they should look out for. But there's also been a number of examples across the political spectrum where we have identified, um, individuals who we know are being particularly targeted sometimes because of their, their ethnicity.

And so we, with full agreement of the political parties, have worked with those individuals to help make them more aware of it, because I do want to stress the majority of, um, targets are unwitting. Yeah. And so we need to help, help shine a light on it. I mean, I guess the thing is, if someone shows up to a candidate and says, here, I'm here to give this donation, that that candidate's not necessarily gonna be in any position to know whether Yeah.

That person has other connections that they should be concerned about. That's exactly it. And you know, from our perspective, one of the benefits of our electoral system is, um, transparency around donations. So that stuff's in the public ar arena, but sometimes that provides good lead information for us to dig a dig a bit deeper.

It's also though individuals who may not be being fully transparent about the nature of their, their motivation connections. Yeah. And so us helping to just broaden understanding of, of that is key, key to managing national look. The point I want to emphasize is having a inclusive democratic system is probably our, our strongest weapon when it comes to countering national security threats.

We're about encouraging that, not trying to limit it. Okay. Tiana, I I Just wanna supplementary, um, you, you talking earlier that you have some people that are motivated by violent extremism who are shopping around. Yeah. So my question is, is where are they, where are they shopping around? Is it, is it online? And if it's online at, at, at, to what extent are you kind of investigating the way that the algorithm can amplify this information and disinformation and push people in?

Yeah. In, in directions that would not necessarily be the most healthy direction On, on the first point. And it very much is online and we think this is a key drive for it. It is just much easier for individuals, particularly marginalized in individuals to be looking online and finding something that they have some sort of affinity with.

And moving on to the, to the next one, look, we aren't the internet place. Neither is GCP. It's not our job to monitor and, um, make determinations about what's, you know, appropriate online content. Where we get focused though is where we see people being mobilized to violence.

So that's where our focus tends to be. And in a forum like this, I probably can't talk too openly about it, but it's often not the stuff that's published in public forums Mm-Hmm. Which is the most concern to us. It's actually getting, getting behind that. We also find, unfortunately that often it's, it's not the people who are most vocal online are the ones who are most concerned.

It's the ones who are quieter and have, um, the more sophisticated, um, what we call opsec operational security are the ones we are concerned about. I do come back to my point though, you know, we are not all seen or known and nor should we be in a democracy like ours. So having the public equipped, um, and confident to raise with us concerns they see is key because they're as likely, if not more likely to see activity of concern as we Thanks.

We've just commemorated the fifth anniversary after, um, uh, you know, the, the terrorist attacks on the Christchurch must died. Yeah. Um, a couple of questions just following on from some of the changes that were put in place in response to the Royal Commission of Inquiry. Um, what progress has been made on the stakeholder or public engagement side of things?

How do you measure that progress? Yeah. And you mentioned that e ethnic communities are particularly monitored and the monitoring is becoming particularly insidious. How important would resilience work in those communities be? Okay. Um, first point in terms of responding to Royal Commission generally, um, there were only a small number of, um, recommendations that related specifically to the service.

But one of those was to produce a annual threat assessment, which we now, now do another, um, was the work around the indicators for violent extremism and getting that out there. So we've, we've progressed those in terms of community engagement. Um, and, you know, I need to acknowledge the work of my predecessor, Rebecca Ridge.

Um, we have developed, um, A series of networks with a range of faith based and ethnic community groups. We meet with them regularly, sometimes at the director general level, sometimes at a, at a, um, sort of a community liaison office level to just hear what they are seeing, hear what their concerns are.

And a big part of that has actually been building confidence about what we do and don't do as agencies. And I, I've really benefited from that and feel significant progress has been made. We don't undertake our own surveying of, of those communities. And one of the reasons is some of the people who we engage with, they would rather it not Mm-Hmm.

Not be known that publicly that we are en engaging with them. We are very supportive of the ongoing work that DPMC have done on monitoring community, sentiment on national security matters, including with regard to the agency. So, I, I don't have metrics for you, but what I can say is, um, the extent to which community engagement is a focus for the agency has increased hugely over the last five years.

And I guess another measure of that is over 20% of our leads now come from community groups, which I guess shows confidence. Yeah. Um, on the point around, um, general community resilience, couldn't, couldn't agree, agree more. And that's obviously a cross, cross government effort. Just one more. Bye. May, um, I'll never forget the mum who sort of talked to me after the attacks happened and said that she could see her teenage son being radicalized.

Yeah. And what were the options for deradicalization? Yeah. Just a question around your point about mitigating threats from terrorism and violent extremism and the work that you're doing with other government agencies. Does that fit into any of that Work? Yes. So, so our agency doesn't have a mandate or a role specifically to, um, work with individuals to be be radicalize them.

Um, you know, and I know there's debate on whether that's even the appropriate term to, to talk about, but to help them make better choices. But we are very supportive of the work that our police colleagues do, our correction colleagues do, our Ms d colleagues. And, you know, sometimes our intelligence can help inform who they should be, should be, um, focusing, focusing on.

So yes, we're supportive of that work, but not in the, not in the lead. Any other questions? Yeah, I was, um, I was just reflecting on the report that came out last week, and I, and I, I think with the attribution that came out today as well, I think many New Zealanders will be surprised, um, and, uh, alarmed that, uh, with foreign interference and, and our, and our national activities and our activities.

And I wanted you to talk a a little bit about the sort of auditing processes that you have put in place given, uh, the report that came out last week with a foreign agency that had marked up somehow. Um, and, um, and, and, uh, and, and what you, what you might see as policy settings that we might need to think about in terms of being able to provide that comfort for New Zealanders in terms of those auditing processes you might have.

But we don't take the public, public trust and confidence for, for granted. It's fundamental for agencies like ours that the public understand what we do and can be assured that we have strong democratic oversight. Andrew will talk specifically about that, that issue.

But, um, there has been a transformation over the last 10 years in terms of oversight in terms of modern legislation, but also in terms of the systems and processes within our two agencies and the culture within the, the agencies. And I think you just only need to look at the, um, I just reports over successive years, which has shown that transformation Information. Uh, yeah, I'd, uh, I'd agree.

I would say that the, uh, the first thing is it shows the value of the work that the Inspector General does. Uh, the Inspector General has, uh, an all access pass across both of our, uh, organizations, gets to see, uh, read anything. Um, and, uh, and in that, uh, report, um, he is fundamentally, uh, finding that there were significant systemic issues going back, uh, to the period where the establishment of that, uh, that system in New Zealand was, uh, getting arranged essentially between about 20, uh, 10, uh, through 2012.

And, um, and turned on in 2013. Uh, that relates to a period, uh, that the Kittridge report finds in 2013, uh, where there were significant systemic issues, uh, in the GCSB after that period. That report sets in, uh, in motion a number of lines of effort that ultimately result in significant legislative change and increasing the Inspector General's powers and uplifting the capability within the GCSB, uh, to, uh, work within, uh, the authorizing environment, uh, and to provide greater, uh, assurance, uh, to the point

where by 2020, uh, it is the GCSB bringing, uh, this system, uh, to light and informing the Inspector General. And in the Inspector General's, uh, report from last week, he concludes, uh, that with all of the changes that have occurred, it would be highly unlikely that something like that could occur again in today's authorizing environment.

A question, uh, you, you talked earlier about, uh, support for, uh, for the Pacific as, as a, as a region. And I, I wanted to know a little bit, bit, a little bit more about that in terms of, 'cause Pacific Island countries also have to navigate, uh, lots of different relationships there. Uh, what could we do better in terms of helping to support and independent Pacific and supporting them to, to navigate those relationships as well?

Yeah. Okay. Um, part of it is us, um, being able to share more information with them through, um, the intelligence that we have access to. We have insights about what's happening in the region, which they may not have the benefit of. So, um, a lot of work that the agencies are involved with now is getting that intelligence into a form that we can share it, but then it's also about providing practical assistance on what they do to, to mitigate the threats.

And we won't be able to talk about specific examples, but it's everything from how we can assist them to get modern, um, legislative and regulatory frameworks so they can make better decisions to quite basic things around how can we, um, how can we just help them to grow the types of capabilities they need.

But, you know, as I've, um, flagged in, in the previous session, you know, just last month the Cook Islands introduced across government, um, protective security framework, which is very much their own, but based on the work we, we had done, you know, you wouldn't have seen something like that even a few years ago.

Okay. Um, probably just, uh, one final question probably for me, which is that, um, I note, you know, quite significantly the improvements in staff attrition over the last year, um, it's been some good progress there considering where we were at this time last year. Um, is that likely to continue and how you feeling about staff attrition in general? Um, we are Everyone okay at the back there?

You know, we are, We are, we are very pleased that we have got over that post covid shock when both agencies were at like 20% turnover. Um, we're now tracking much closer to, to 10, 10%. Yeah. Um, what we are also though aware of is that, you know, our agencies have gone through a period of substantial Yes.

Growth. We are not expecting that either agency's gonna be growing much in the future. So we have a real focus on making sure that we are deploying the staff, that we've gotten the most effective, um, and Optim, um, optimal way. And so, you know, we are being disciplined around our recruitment at the, at the moment.

Um, having said that, the agencies are the biggest they've ever, they've ever been. We just need to make sure we continue to deliver value to the public of New Zealand with, um, the resources that have invested in us. Great. Judith, I just really wanted to say, um, thank you for the work you do. Mm. And tell the teams, um, particularly impressed with the work that you've done with the Shehar, that terrible, uh, terrorist attack in Christchurch.

And I was very impressed, and I think those of us who were there, uh, at the fifth, um, five year anniversary were so impressed that, uh, everyone knew you, everyone was able to, um, talk to you and, uh, they knew actually you're on their side. So it was great. Thank you for that, minister. I'll pass it on.

But both of our, both of us are fortunate in that we lead great teams. Indeed. Hey, listen, can I say to both of you, thank you so much, um, for, uh, spending time with us today and, and answering our questions. Really appreciate it. With that, uh, I'll close this public session. Uh, we'll have a private session wrap up and then we'll.
